refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from mission context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Atomicity requires separating these concerns. CR-NGHE-005.1 focuses on the derivation act—the logical transformation from mission inputs (worksite conditions, operational goals, constraints) to performance targets. This is solution-neutral: it does not prescribe *how* derivation occurs (algorithm, manual process, tool) or *where* records are stored. Recording and lifecycle management are separate functional concerns.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a defined worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints), the system produces a documented set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that are traceable back to mission inputs and satisfy acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance requirements with traceability to worksite mission inputs.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance requirement baselines and calibration updates.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall manage performance requirement changes in accordance with worksite operational policies.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Establish a parameterized mission-context intake process that captures worksite operational profile (hazard class, duty cycle, environmental conditions, safety constraints) and maps each input to a corresponding performance target via a documented traceability matrix, with version control for baseline snapshots.

DP-B: Implement a hierarchical performance requirement derivation engine that accepts worksite mission inputs, applies domain-specific inference rules (safety-driven, duty-cycle-driven, environmental-driven), and outputs a structured performance specification document with explicit linkage to source constraints.

DP-C: Design a dual-layer requirement capture system: Layer 1 (intake) collects raw worksite context (safety plan excerpts, operational policies, environmental data); Layer 2 (derivation) applies a rule-based or model-based transformation to produce performance targets, with audit trail for each derivation step.

DP-D: Create a performance target repository with versioned baseline records, where each baseline is tagged with the worksite mission context snapshot that generated it, and provide a change-history mechanism to track calibration updates and their justification.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C (dual-layer requirement capture and derivation) combined with DP-D (versioned baseline repository). DP-C separates concerns (intake vs. derivation logic) and supports traceability; DP-D ensures acceptance criterion compliance (documented targets, history, calibration tracking). Together they form a cohesive, loosely-coupled solution.

potential_sources: Risk: Tight coupling between intake schema and derivation rules—if worksite input format changes, rule engine may break. Mitigation: Define intake schema as a stable contract; derivation rules reference schema fields by semantic name, not position.

Risk: Baseline repository and derivation engine may share state (e.g., current active baseline). Mitigation: Repository acts as read-only source of truth; derivation engine queries it but does not mutate it directly.

Risk: Audit trail and versioning logic could become entangled with derivation logic. Mitigation: Implement versioning as a cross-cutting concern (wrapper or middleware) independent of derivation algorithm.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C and DP-D are logically independent: intake/derivation can be redesigned without affecting baseline storage, and vice versa. Coupling is managed via well-defined interfaces (intake schema, derivation output format, repository query API).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite mission context is stable enough to be captured in a structured intake form. Risk: Highly dynamic or ad-hoc worksites may require frequent re-intake. Mitigation: Design intake process to support incremental updates and re-derivation triggers.

Assumption: Domain-specific inference rules (safety-driven, duty-cycle-driven, etc.) can be codified and maintained. Risk: Rules may be implicit in expert judgment and difficult to formalize. Mitigation: Engage domain experts early; document rule rationale; plan for rule versioning and review cycles.

Assumption: Traceability matrix can be automatically generated from intake inputs and derivation rules. Risk: Some performance targets may require manual expert judgment beyond rule-based derivation. Mitigation: Hybrid approach—rules generate candidates; experts review and override if needed; all overrides logged.

Assumption: Baseline history and calibration updates are infrequent enough to manage manually or via simple versioning. Risk: High-frequency updates or concurrent modifications could create merge conflicts. Mitigation: Define clear ownership and approval workflow for baseline changes.

Assumption: Acceptance criteria (documented targets, traceability, history) can be satisfied by a single integrated system. Risk: Distributed or legacy systems may already hold parts of this data. Mitigation: Design repository as a facade or integration layer that can aggregate data from multiple sources.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR-NGHE-005.1 to explicitly distinguish between: (1) intake of worksite mission context, (2) derivation of performance targets from context, and (3) management of baseline versions and calibration history. This separation clarifies the scope and allows independent DP evolution. Proposed refined FRs: FR-NGHE-005.1a (Capture worksite mission context), FR-NGHE-005.1b (Derive performance targets from context), FR-NGHE-005.1c (Maintain versioned baseline and calibration history). Each FR should map to a distinct DP and IM, reducing coupling and improving testability.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized requirement capture, derivation logic, versioned baseline repository, and acceptance criterion management. Owns the dual-layer intake-vs-derivation separation and maintains the versioned baseline with full traceability history and calibration tracking.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational telemetry and sensor data to inform requirement derivation and acceptance criterion calibration.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs derived task specifications and acceptance criteria to Vehicle and Teleoperation for execution and validation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and alerts; outputs safety-compliant acceptance criteria and baseline versions to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides versioned baseline repository access and requirement traceability queries to distributed stakeholders; receives baseline synchronization requests.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. DP-C (dual-layer intake vs. derivation) and DP-D (versioned baseline with traceability) are core to NGHE's mission of requirement management and acceptance criterion derivation. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry (input calibration), Tasking (output specs), SafetyAlert (constraint integration), and Network Link (repository access) cross the boundary. No duplication of logic across other modules.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a consumer of derived requirements and acceptance criteria, not an owner of requirement capture or baseline versioning. Placing DP-C and DP-D here would scatter requirement logic across execution platforms, violating separation of concerns and creating tight coupling between requirement derivation and vehicle control.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is an interface/control layer that consumes tasking and acceptance criteria. Embedding requirement capture and baseline versioning here would conflate human-in-the-loop control with requirement management, creating high diffusion and poor cohesion.

Weak fit. Safety Services owns safety constraint validation and alert generation, but does not own the broader requirement capture, derivation, or baseline versioning logic. Placing DP-C and DP-D here would fragment requirement management and create tight coupling between safety-specific logic and general requirement traceability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP-C and DP-D are best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) NGHE is the natural owner of requirement intake, derivation logic, and acceptance criterion management; (2) the dual-layer separation (intake vs. derivation) and versioned baseline repository are core to NGHE's functional identity; (3) external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal, supporting loose coupling; (4) no other module has the functional scope or ownership authority to manage requirement traceability and baseline versioning without creating diffusion and violating separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck if requirement derivation logic is not sufficiently modularized internally (e.g., separate sub-modules for intake, derivation, versioning). Mitigation: Ensure internal cohesion through clear sub-module boundaries within NGHE.
Risk: Telemetry input for calibration may introduce tight coupling if telemetry schema changes frequently. Mitigation: Define a stable telemetry contract and version it independently.

Risk: SafetyAlert integration may create circular dependencies if Safety Services queries the baseline repository for historical safety criteria. Mitigation: Establish unidirectional flow: NGHE outputs safety-compliant criteria to Safety Services; Safety Services provides alerts back to NGHE for re-derivation.

Risk: Network Link interface for distributed repository access may introduce latency or consistency issues. Mitigation: Define clear versioning and synchronization semantics (e.g., eventual consistency, version locks).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. DP-C and DP-D are well-suited to NGHE Solution and do not require implementation-specific adjustments. However, recommend clarifying internal sub-module structure within NGHE to separate: (1) Requirement Intake Module (handles raw input, validation), (2) Derivation Engine Module (applies logic to derive acceptance criteria), and (3) Baseline Repository Module (manages versioning, history, calibration). This internal decomposition will improve cohesion and reduce internal diffusion while maintaining the external interface contract.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (dual-layer requirement capture and derivation) and DP-D (versioned baseline repository) directly and sufficiently address FR CR-NGHE-005.1. DP-C separates intake from derivation logic, enabling traceable derivation from worksite mission context. DP-D ensures documented performance targets with full history and calibration tracking. Together, they form a cohesive solution that satisfies both the functional requirement and the acceptance criteria (documented targets traceable to worksite inputs, maintained baseline history).

diffusion_risks: NGHE Solution containment score of 0.95 is excellent, but internal diffusion risk exists if DP-C and DP-D are not further decomposed into sub-modules. Recommend explicit internal boundaries: Requirement Intake Module, Derivation Engine Module, and Baseline Repository Module to prevent logic scatter within NGHE.
Telemetry interface (inbound) for calibration input introduces coupling risk if telemetry schema evolves. Mitigation: Define and version a stable telemetry contract independently.

SafetyAlert bidirectional interface may create circular dependency if Safety Services queries baseline repository for historical safety criteria. Mitigation: Enforce unidirectional flow—NGHE outputs safety-compliant criteria; Safety Services provides alerts for re-derivation only.

Network Link interface for distributed repository access introduces potential latency and consistency risks. Mitigation: Define explicit versioning and synchronization semantics (e.g., eventual consistency, version locks, conflict resolution).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary for DP-C and DP-D. Vehicle (0.15 containment, 0.85 diffusion), Teleoperation (0.2 containment, 0.8 diffusion), and Safety Services (0.35 containment, 0.65 diffusion) are all poor fits—they are consumers of derived requirements, not owners of requirement capture and baseline versioning. Placing DP-C or DP-D in any of these modules would scatter requirement logic, violate separation of concerns, and create tight coupling. NGHE's functional scope (requirement intake, derivation, acceptance criterion management) and natural authority over traceability and baseline versioning make it the only viable owner. External interfaces are well-defined and minimal, supporting loose coupling.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criteria are directly verifiable: (1) 'documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs' can be verified by inspecting NGHE's baseline repository for traceability links from worksite mission context to derived targets; (2) 'maintains a history of baselines/calibration updates' can be verified by auditing the versioned baseline repository for complete change history and calibration records. DP-D (versioned baseline repository) provides the mechanism for both verifications.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR CR-NGHE-005.1 is allocated entirely to NGHE Solution via DP-C and DP-D. No ambiguity exists regarding which module owns requirement derivation and baseline management. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined, with clear input/output semantics. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are inputs to the derivation logic (DP-C), not owned by NGHE but consumed and integrated into derived acceptance criteria.

recommended_revisions: Decompose NGHE Solution internally into three sub-modules: (1) Requirement Intake Module—validates and normalizes raw worksite mission context inputs; (2) Derivation Engine Module—applies derivation logic to produce acceptance criteria; (3) Baseline Repository Module—manages versioning, history, and calibration tracking. This internal structure will reduce internal diffusion and improve cohesion while maintaining the external interface contract.

Define and document a stable Telemetry contract (schema, versioning, update frequency) to decouple NGHE from telemetry schema evolution. Version the contract independently and establish a deprecation policy.

Establish explicit unidirectional flow for SafetyAlert integration: NGHE outputs safety-compliant acceptance criteria to Safety Services; Safety Services provides alerts back to NGHE as inputs for re-derivation. Document this flow to prevent circular dependencies.

Define versioning and synchronization semantics for the Network Link interface (e.g., eventual consistency model, version locks, conflict resolution strategy) to mitigate latency and consistency risks in distributed repository access.
No revision to DP-C or DP-D selection is necessary. Both are well-suited to NGHE Solution and satisfy FR CR-NGHE-005.1 with high fidelity.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. However, the DP-IM gate explicitly recommends internal decomposition of NGHE Solution into three sub-modules (Requirement Intake, Derivation Engine, Baseline Repository) to reduce internal diffusion risk and improve cohesion. Given the high safety criticality of this requirement (FR CR-NGHE-005.1 governs acceptance criteria derivation—a safety-critical function), and the clear architectural guidance from the gate analysis, proceeding to next-level decomposition is justified. This decomposition will harden the internal architecture, establish explicit module boundaries, and reduce the risk of logic scatter within NGHE. The recommended revisions are architectural refinements, not fundamental DP/IM rejections, so ZIGZAG_DOWN is appropriate rather than REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL.
next_level_sub_frs: FR CR-NGHE-005.1.1: Validate and normalize raw worksite mission context inputs (safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints) to a canonical internal representation suitable for derivation logic.

FR CR-NGHE-005.1.2: Apply derivation logic to transform normalized worksite mission context into acceptance criteria (performance targets, safety thresholds, operational constraints) that are traceable to source inputs.
FR CR-NGHE-005.1.3: Manage versioning, history, and calibration tracking of derived acceptance criteria baselines to support audit, rollback, and compliance verification.

revise.why: The current IM (NGHE Solution as a monolithic module) is correct in ownership and containment, but lacks internal structure. Decompose NGHE Solution into three sub-modules corresponding to the three sub-FRs: (1) Requirement Intake Module (validates/normalizes inputs), (2) Derivation Engine Module (applies derivation logic), (3) Baseline Repository Module (manages versioning/history). This internal decomposition reduces diffusion risk, improves cohesion, and establishes clear internal interfaces. The external IM boundary (NGHE Solution) remains unchanged; only internal packaging is refined.

assumptions: Worksite mission context (safety plan, operational policies, environmental constraints) is provided as structured input to NGHE Solution and does not require real-time updates during operation.
Derivation logic is deterministic and can be versioned independently of baseline repository state.
Telemetry inputs for calibration are infrequent (not continuous streaming) and can be batched for derivation re-runs.
SafetyAlert feedback from Safety Services is asynchronous and triggers re-derivation only when safety criteria change, not on every alert.
Network Link interface supports eventual consistency semantics; strict real-time synchronization is not required for distributed repository access.
open_risks: Telemetry schema evolution: If telemetry contract is not explicitly versioned and deprecated, NGHE may break on schema changes. Mitigation: Define stable telemetry contract with independent versioning and deprecation policy (recommended in gate analysis).

SafetyAlert circular dependency: If Safety Services queries baseline repository for historical safety criteria, a circular dependency may emerge. Mitigation: Enforce unidirectional flow—NGHE outputs criteria; Safety Services provides alerts as inputs only (recommended in gate analysis).

Distributed repository consistency: Network Link interface for distributed baseline repository access introduces latency and consistency risks. Mitigation: Define explicit versioning and synchronization semantics (e.g., eventual consistency, version locks, conflict resolution) (recommended in gate analysis).

Internal module coupling: If Requirement Intake Module, Derivation Engine Module, and Baseline Repository Module are not cleanly separated by explicit interfaces, internal diffusion risk persists. Mitigation: Define and document internal module interfaces (contracts) during next-level decomposition.

Safety criticality of derivation logic: Acceptance criteria derived by DP-C are safety-critical. If derivation logic contains errors or is not properly validated, derived criteria may be unsafe. Mitigation: Establish formal verification or extensive testing of derivation logic; trace all safety-critical criteria back to source inputs and safety plan.
evidence_needed: Detailed specification of Requirement Intake Module: input schema, normalization rules, validation logic, error handling.
Detailed specification of Derivation Engine Module: derivation algorithm, traceability mechanism, handling of conflicting or ambiguous inputs, safety-critical decision points.
Detailed specification of Baseline Repository Module: versioning scheme, history retention policy, calibration tracking mechanism, rollback procedures, distributed synchronization semantics.
Telemetry contract specification: schema definition, versioning policy, deprecation timeline, update frequency, error handling.
SafetyAlert integration specification: unidirectional flow diagram, alert types, re-derivation triggers, feedback loop closure mechanism.
Network Link interface specification: synchronization semantics, version locks, conflict resolution strategy, latency bounds, consistency guarantees.
Traceability matrix: mapping from worksite mission context inputs to derived acceptance criteria, with explicit links to safety plan and operational policies.
Validation and verification plan for derivation logic, including formal methods or extensive testing for safety-critical criteria.
Audit trail specification: how NGHE records and exposes baseline history and calibration updates for compliance verification.
